Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic but it worked for me. I'm a very visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams, mind maps, and color-coded notes rather than just reading through materials. I typically studied in intensive bursts—maybe 25 hours spread across the week, but concentrated around when I felt most focused. I didn't always attend lectures because I found I could learn better from visual resources on my own time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurricular commitments that sometimes took priority, so I'd adjust my study blocks around those. When exams approached, I'd lock myself in and study intensively, but during quieter weeks I might spread things out more evenly. Deadlines were really what shaped my rhythm more than any fixed schedule.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download new materials. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts or updates that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in frequently just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important—there was always this underlying fear of falling behind even when I was doing well. The anxiety of missing something kept me checking regularly.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic—like checking my phone first thing in the morning. I'd log in during breakfast or between my extracurricular activities just to scan for updates. It became part of my routine because I realized early on that staying connected helped manage my anxiety about the course. Even if I wasn't actively studying, knowing what was coming up made me feel more in control.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me because of my visual learning style—I watched them multiple times, probably totaling over 70 minutes of viewing. I also found the discussion forums helpful because seeing how other students interpreted concepts gave me different perspectives. Any resource with diagrams, flowcharts, or visual explanations immediately grabbed my attention and helped concepts stick better than text-heavy materials.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped or minimally engaged with reading-heavy PDFs and text documents. They just didn't work with how my brain processes information. I'd download them intending to read later but often ended up converting the key points into visual notes instead of reading them thoroughly. Some supplementary resources also felt redundant if I'd already grasped the concept from videos.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam content or if it helped clarify something I was struggling with. Visual resources always felt valuable because they matched how I learn best. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers—those 23 peer interactions I had really helped solidify my understanding. If something felt like busywork without clear learning outcomes, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which actually made me more selective about them. Required activities I'd complete regardless of how I felt about them, but optional ones I'd only do if they genuinely seemed helpful. Sometimes this meant I'd skip optional readings but spend extra time on optional practice problems. The pressure was different—optional felt like a choice I was making for myself.
Q9: Absolutely—a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work with them on my tablet where I could annotate and draw all over them. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts and created physical flashcards with diagrams. My textbook had way more annotations than the LMS would ever show. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any trackable platform.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from memory, testing myself with flashcards, or explaining concepts out loud to myself—none of which showed up anywhere. Sometimes I'd study with classmates in person, working through problems together on a whiteboard. My exam score of nearly 98% reflects learning that mostly happened in these invisible ways. The LMS saw me download things, but not the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then that material lived on my devices for repeated study. I probably watched some lecture videos three or four times offline while only showing one view in the system. My tablet became my actual study hub—full of annotated PDFs and screenshots that I'd review constantly without ever logging back into the LMS.
Q12: There were definitely periods when fear and anxiety made it harder to engage. Sometimes I'd feel scared about whether I was understanding things correctly, especially when my assignment scores weren't reflecting my exam performance. When extracurriculars got intense or when I was feeling overwhelmed, I'd pull back from the LMS and just focus on what felt manageable. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of this, but the scared feelings didn't fully go away.
Q13: Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely affected my LMS usage—there were weeks where I barely logged in because I was stretched thin elsewhere. Fatigue was real too; sometimes I'd log in intending to engage but just couldn't focus. Confusion about certain topics sometimes made me avoid those sections temporarily until I felt ready to tackle them. My medium resources meant I couldn't always access help when I needed it most.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. When anxiety was high, I'd go through the motions of studying without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd open multiple resources just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually engaged. Those sessions felt hollow even though they probably showed up as activity in the logs.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my assignment completion and scores were lower than my exam performance. The fear of not doing them perfectly sometimes paralyzed me into avoidance. I knew they were important, but when I felt scared or overwhelmed, it was easier to focus on studying concepts I felt more confident about. My resilience helped me eventually push through, but the delay was real.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me—they created the urgency I needed to overcome my avoidance tendencies. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I might have explored more leisurely, but with them, I became very strategic about what to prioritize. Sometimes the deadline pressure helped me focus; other times it just amplified my anxiety and fear.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more, but critical feedback sometimes made me scared to continue in that area. When my assignment scores came back lower than expected, I'd feel discouraged even though my exam preparation was going well. The disconnect between my assignment performance and my actual understanding was frustrating. I tried to use feedback constructively, but emotionally it was hard.
Q18: The course structure with its mix of videos, readings, and discussions actually worked well for my visual learning style when I could choose my path. Having multiple resource types meant I could gravitate toward what worked for me. However, the attendance expectations didn't match how I learned best—I performed better studying independently than sitting in lectures. The flexibility to engage with materials on my own terms was crucial for my success.
Q19: Visual resources and well-organized modules made engagement much easier for me. When materials were clearly labeled and I could see the learning pathway, I felt less anxious. What made it harder was when important information was buried in text-heavy documents or when the connection between activities and assessments wasn't clear. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged the peer interactions that helped my learning.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it—clicking through materials hoping something clicks but not being able to produce work that demonstrates learning. Or they might be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism that makes them over-prepare but freeze when it comes to actually submitting. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and repeatedly returning to materials trying to grasp concepts. It's not always about effort matching output.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well. My attendance was only about 34% and my assignment completion was around 50%, but I scored nearly 98% on my exam. I learned effectively through offline study, visual note-making, and self-testing that didn't show up in the LMS. Some students just learn differently and don't need to be constantly visible in the system to be genuinely engaged with the material. The metrics can miss the depth of learning happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing of engagement, and which resources students gravitate toward. They capture login frequency and basic participation metrics. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline study, emotional state during learning, and whether someone is actually understanding versus just clicking through. They can't see the mind maps I drew, the concepts I explained to friends, or the fear I felt while studying.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me—not just completing tasks but actually understanding concepts deeply enough to apply them. It's about being mentally present and curious, asking questions even if just to myself, and connecting new information to what I already know. For me, engagement also means finding ways to make the material visual and memorable. It's an internal process that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my emotional journey—the fear and anxiety I battled while still pushing through to achieve distinction. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent creating visual study materials or studying with peers outside the platform. It can't see that my low attendance was a deliberate choice because I learned better independently, not a sign of disengagement. Most importantly, it wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept going despite feeling scared, and how the gap between my assignment scores and exam performance reflected different types of challenges, not lack of effort.
